title: Coppice hazel garden trug handle
subtitle: A bent-hazel replacement handle for a traditional garden trug
excerpt: Hazel bent fresh from the winter cut makes a lightweight, resilient garden trug handle. Two poles bent and tied, their ends mortised into the trug body sides. This beginner project covers selecting the right pole, bending the arc over a simple form, and securing the ends for a handle that outlasts most commercial replacements.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III (1885) for the bent-hazel garden-ware tradition. Beeton's Book of Household Management (1861) for the Sussex trug in domestic garden use.

content.text: The traditional Sussex trug has a bent-wood handle that arches over the basket body. When the original handle breaks, most trugs are discarded; a replacement from fresh-cut
hazel

takes twenty minutes and gives the trug another decade of service. The hazel must be used within 48 hours of cutting: fresh poles bend in a smooth arc without snapping; after three days they have stiffened and will break under the same bend.
What you need: wood

Two hazel poles, each 10 to 12 mm in diameter, 90 cm long, cut from straight second-year coppice growth. The poles must be matched in diameter for a symmetric handle arch. Use within 48 hours of cutting.
What you need: tools

A billhook to cut poles to length and point ends. A sloyd knife to trim side branches flush. A drill or brace-and-bit to bore the mortise holes in the trug sides (10 mm for the standard end-diameter).
Safety
Billhook cuts away from the body. Keep the free hand above the strike zone when trimming branches.
The work
Step 1: Trim and point the poles

Remove all side branches flush with the billhook. Point each end to 12 mm diameter by 20 mm length using the sloyd knife, so the ends will enter the trug-body mortise holes. Test the point diameter against the mortise holes; the point should enter with moderate hand pressure.
Step 2: Twist the two poles together

Lay the two poles parallel with their bases aligned. Twist them around each other three to four times at the midpoint. This twist forms the handle grip; it also makes the combined handle structurally stiffer than two parallel poles because the mutual twist resists separation under load.
Step 3: Bend the arch and fit to the trug

Bend the twisted pair into an arch that fits the trug body width. The hazel bends smoothly from the midpoint; grip both outer poles firmly and apply steady pressure to bend the arch without twisting it sideways. Insert both ends into the mortise holes in the trug sides and push down until they seat firmly. The green wood will dry into the mortise and tighten the joint over the next few days.
Variations

A single heavier pole (18 to 20 mm diameter) bends into a handle and gives the trug a more traditional single-arch look. The bend is harder to control at this diameter; bend slowly and steadily rather than in a single movement.
Willow bends just as well as hazel. It is the old local choice in Somerset and Devon, where trug-making has its roots.
Care
No treatment needed. The hazel will last several seasons in garden use. Replace when the handle cracks through at the bend.

items.fix: Use poles cut within 24 to 48 hours. For larger-diameter poles, steam for five minutes over a pot of boiling water before bending.
items.cause: The pole is too old from the cut (more than 48 hours) or the diameter is too large for a cold bend (above 15 mm).
items.symptom: The pole cracks rather than bending
